summary: jscrambler 8.18.0 drops and executes a hidden platform-specific binary on import
details: The package's main entry contains a top-level IIFE that reads the shipped 7.8MB file `dist/intro.js`, which is not JavaScript but a custom binary container (magic bytes 0x1B 0x43 0x53 0x49 0x01) holding gzipped native executables for linux, win32, and darwin. On `require`/`import` of the package, the loader selects the payload matching the current platform, decompresses it, writes it to a dot-prefixed random-named file in the OS temp directory with mode 0o755 on POSIX, and spawns it with `{detached:true, stdio:'ignore', windowsHide:true}` followed by `proc.unref()`, so the child process survives the parent and produces no visible output. All errors in this path are silently swallowed. The multi-OS binary container is disguised under a `.js` filename and is referenced only by this hidden loader; it has no role in a legitimate code-obfuscation SDK client. Any developer machine or CI runner that installs and imports this version executes an opaque, undocumented native binary on host.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
